title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt:
The local council has asked students at your college to write a report on what young people like and dislike about living in your town or city. Include information about what facilities young people appreciate, what they feel is missing, and recommendations for improvement.

Write your report.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Young People's Views on City Life in Northfield

Introduction
This report is based on interviews and surveys conducted with 120 students aged 16–22 living in Northfield, with the aim of informing the council's youth development strategy.

What Young People Value
Respondents highlighted several positive aspects of city life. The public transport network was praised for being affordable and reliable, and the range of cultural events — including music festivals and art exhibitions — was widely appreciated. The central library and its free Wi-Fi were also mentioned positively.

Areas for Improvement
The most common complaint was the lack of affordable leisure facilities specifically aimed at young people. Many respondents noted that sports centres and cinema tickets have become too expensive. Several students also expressed concerns about safety in the city centre at night and the limited green spaces available for relaxation.

Recommendations
The council should consider introducing a youth discount card for local amenities, investing in well-lit public parks, and developing more affordable after-school activity programmes. These measures would significantly improve young people's quality of life in Northfield.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Jordan has just moved to a new city and is finding it difficult to settle in. Write an email to Jordan offering advice on how to meet new people, suggesting things to do in the city, and sharing your own experience of adapting to a new place.

Write your email to Jordan.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Jordan,

I'm sorry to hear you're finding it tough to settle in — I completely understand that feeling! When I moved to a new city two years ago, I felt exactly the same. But things did get better, and I'm sure they will for you too.

The best advice I can give is to say yes to everything, at least at first. Accept every invitation, join every club, attend every event — even if it feels uncomfortable. I joined a running club in my first week and within a month I had a whole social group.

As for things to do in the city, I'd suggest exploring local markets, checking out free museum evenings, and looking for community events on Facebook or Eventbrite. Most cities have far more going on than you'd initially think.

Most importantly, be patient with yourself. Making genuine friends takes time, and that's completely normal. You're braver than you think for making this move in the first place!

I'm always here if you want to chat.

Hugs,
Beatrice

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'City life or country life — which is better? Write an article exploring the advantages and disadvantages of both, and share your personal preference and the reasons for it.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The Great Debate: City Life vs Country Life

Where you choose to live shapes almost everything about your daily existence — from the air you breathe to the people you meet. The debate between city life and country life is one of the oldest and most personal there is.

Cities offer unparalleled opportunity. Job prospects, cultural events, diverse communities, and excellent transport links make urban life dynamic and stimulating. For ambitious young people especially, cities can feel like the centre of everything that matters.

However, city life has its costs. Noise, pollution, high housing prices, and the sense of anonymity that comes with living among millions of strangers can be deeply exhausting. Many city dwellers dream of escaping to somewhere quieter.

The countryside offers peace, natural beauty, and a stronger sense of community. But for young people, the lack of professional opportunities and limited social life can quickly become frustrating.

Personally, I have made my choice: I love the energy of city life too much to leave. But I make sure to spend time in nature regularly — because that, I have discovered, is the best of both worlds.
